match: Argentina vs England, World Cup quarterfinal
title: Maradona's "Hand of God"
description: Diego Maradona punched the ball into the net with his hand during a quarterfinal against England.
decision: Goal awarded — the referee and linesman did not see the handball and the goal stood.
technology_available: None — no video assistant referee, no goal-line or handball review technology existed.
rule_at_time: Law 12 handball decisions relied entirely on the on-field officials' live view.

match: England vs Germany, World Cup round of 16
title: Lampard's disallowed goal
description: Frank Lampard's shot struck the crossbar and clearly crossed the goal line before bouncing out, but the goal was not awarded.
decision: No goal awarded — the referee and linesman missed the ball crossing the line in real time.
technology_available: None — goal-line technology was not yet approved for use at the World Cup.
rule_at_time: Goal-line decisions relied entirely on the on-field officials' live view, with no review mechanism.

match: Uruguay vs Ghana, World Cup quarterfinal
title: Suárez's goal-line handball
description: Luis Suárez deliberately handled a goal-bound header on his own goal line in the final seconds of extra time.
decision: Penalty awarded and Suárez sent off; Ghana missed the resulting penalty kick.
technology_available: None for the handball review itself — the referee was positioned to see the incident live and ruled immediately under existing law without needing replay.
rule_at_time: Law 12 allows a direct red card and penalty for denying an obvious goal by deliberate handball.

match: France vs Australia, World Cup group stage
title: First VAR-awarded penalty in World Cup history
description: Antoine Griezmann went down under a challenge in the box; the on-field referee initially waved play on.
decision: Penalty awarded after a pitchside VAR review reversed the no-call.
technology_available: Video Assistant Referee (VAR) pitchside monitor review, used for the first time at a World Cup.
rule_at_time: VAR protocol allowed review of penalty decisions for clear and obvious errors.

match: Argentina vs Saudi Arabia, World Cup group stage
title: Three offside goals disallowed in one half
description: Argentina had three goals ruled out for offside in the first half, each by very tight margins.
decision: All three goals disallowed after VAR review using semi-automated offside technology.
technology_available: Semi-automated offside technology with limb-tracking cameras, used for the first time at a World Cup.
rule_at_time: Law 11 offside, measured to centimeter-level precision rather than relying on a linesman's flag.

match: Japan vs Spain, World Cup group stage
title: Japan's 1.88 mm goal-line call
description: Before Japan's second goal, the ball appeared to cross the byline for a goal kick before being cut back into play.
decision: Goal awarded after VAR used multi-angle ball-tracking to determine the ball retained 1.88 mm of contact with the line.
technology_available: Multi-camera ball-tracking review, used to make a sub-centimeter determination invisible to the naked eye.
rule_at_time: A ball is out of play only once it has wholly crossed the line; technology allowed a precision check standard officiating could not.
